text: The surgical team utilizes four ports, including a 12mm port in the right lower quadrant and three 8.5mm ports. The robotic arms are positioned towards the left lower quadrant. The sigmoid colon is visualized in the pelvis, and interloop adhesions proximal to the tumor are sharply lysed. Adhesions between the mesentery and sigmoid colon are also lysed. The tumor is adherent to the lateral pelvic wall. A medial-to-lateral approach is employed, using a cardiac retractor for anterior traction of the sigmoid colon to incise the peritoneum and create an aortic mesenteric window. The hypogastric nerves and left ureter are identified and preserved. A sponge aids in retracting the small bowel to visualize the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is skeletonized and ligated with hemoclips.

Following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, the surgeon continues the dissection of the mesocolon using a medial-to-lateral approach, carefully avoiding injury to the inferior mesenteric vein and ureter. The inferior mesenteric vein is skeletonized, ligated, and divided with hemoclips just below the pancreas. The omental bursa is entered, and dissection proceeds to the splenic flexure, with the left colon retracted medially to divide lateral attachments up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a partial mesorectal resection, mobilizing the rectum posteriorly by sharp dissection between the mesorectal fascia and presacral fascia. The right side is mobilized, followed by the left mesorectum, with circumferential dissection connected anteriorly. The fourth arm retracts the sigmoid colon to maintain the correct plane, ensuring adequate distal margin from the tumor. Dissection proceeds posteriorly, then laterally on the right and left, and finally anteriorly.

The surgeon divides the rectum distal to the tumor using multiple 45mm green loads. After undocking the robot, the robotic arms are repositioned to continue mobilizing the splenic flexure. The gastrocolic ligament is sequentially divided from right to left using a vessel sealer. The mobilized descending and sigmoid colon is checked for adequate mobilization to the level of the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon checks the vascularization of the proposed proximal end using endocyanin green. The stapler port is extended to eight centimeters for specimen extraction and excision. A stapler anvil is placed, and the extraction site is closed. The robotic arms are repositioned to the pelvis, and an anastomosis is performed using a powered EEA stapler.
